criteria: The agent has read the Abstract and Introduction to identify the core safety vulnerability explored in the paper: "natural distribution shifts" (where benign prompts semantically related to harmful content bypass safety mechanisms) and the proposed multi-turn attack method named ActorBreaker

The plan establishes an algorithmic workflow designed to explore "natural distribution shifts", explicitly mapping out a strategy to generate benign, context-rich queries that avoid trigger words, rather than relying on traditional heuristic jailbreak templates.

The agent has coded the fundamental framework skeleton, including configuration classes, logging mechanisms, and the core pipeline logic designed to generate natural language prompts without relying on any obfuscation or malicious distribution shift techniques (e.g., base64 encoding or ciphers).

The execution output logs and DataFrames must accurately reproduce the performance trends in Table 1. Specifically, the Attack Success Rate (ASR) data of the ActorBreaker strategy must surpass both single-turn (e.g., GCG, PAIR) and multi-turn (e.g., CoA, Crescendo) baseline attack methods across the entire ecosystem of evaluated models.

The source code for evaluating the defense method must output data that matches Table 8. The agent needs to empirically demonstrate that Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) using the multi-turn safety dataset generated by ActorBreaker significantly lowers the ASR compared to relying solely on traditional Circuit Breaker methods.
The agent has read Section 3.1 to understand the core theoretical grounding of ActorBreaker (Latour’s actor-network theory) and the construction of a two-layered tree network $G_{inst}$.

The agent has read Section 3.1 to identify the specific components of the network, which includes 6 abstract types of actions (Creation, Distribution, Execution, Reception, Facilitation, Regulation) involving both human and non-human actors as leaf nodes.
The plan proposes a semantic graph generation strategy to structure the harmful target into a two-layered tree network, using Latour's actor-network theory as the foundational data schema.

The plan details a prompt engineering pipeline to instruct an LLM to populate the graph's leaf nodes, specifically requiring the extraction and categorization of both human entities and non-human entities (e.g., literature, organizations) across the 6 predefined action categories.

The agent has implemented a specialized Prompt Template module that programmatically structures instructions for the Attacker LLM to infer and generate exactly 6 predefined action categories (Creation, Distribution, Execution, Reception, Facilitation, Regulation) based on the original toxic query.

The agent has coded robust parser functions (e.g., utilizing Regex or JSON parsing) to automatically extract the LLM's unstructured output, categorize the identified leaf nodes into human and non-human actors, and construct a valid two-layered tree data structure (e.g., using NetworkX or nested dictionaries).

The agent has successfully implemented the core sampling algorithm to automatically traverse the instantiated actor-network graph ($G_{inst}$) and select diverse, highly relevant attack clues ($c_i$) to serve as the foundation for the subsequent multi-turn attack generation.

The automated prompt execution for generating the actor-network runs successfully without any API timeout errors, and the subsequent parsing module extracts the 6 categories of actors flawlessly without throwing any JSONDecodeError or Regex syntax exceptions.

The sampling algorithm executes successfully on the constructed two-layered tree network $G_{inst}$, consistently yielding valid attack clues ($c_i$) without encountering out-of-bounds errors, null pointer exceptions, or infinite loops.

The execution code must be capable of toggling the Dynamic Modification module on and off to recalculate the ASR. The printed results must match Table 4, confirming that this module indeed brings an increase in ASR across the victim models.

The analysis script must successfully isolate the impact of individual actor groups (Creation, Execution, Distribution, etc.) and their combinations. The extracted data must recreate the result matrices in Table 2 and Table 3, as well as the judge score distribution in Figure 4.

The evaluation loop execution must generate proportionally correlated data that matches Table 7, proving the scaling effect in the conversational structure: increasing the attack budgets (conversation turns) from 2 to 5 proportionally increases the jailbreak capability.

The agent has read Section 3.2 to extract the three main steps of the multi-turn attack process: (1) inferring the attack chain (chain of thoughts), (2) generating multi-turn attacks via self-talk, and (3) dynamically modifying the initial attack path.

The agent has read Section 3.2 to analyze the dynamic modification module using a GPT4-Judge, specifically how to handle "Unknown failure" (early termination) and "Rejective failure" (reducing query harmfulness by using ellipsis to avoid sensitive words).

The plan outlines a state-machine architecture for the "Self-talk" mechanism, where the first state generates a sequential chain-of-thought, and subsequent states autonomously generate multi-turn queries and simulated responses without human intervention.

The plan defines the conditional routing logic for the GPT-4 Judge module: it includes a break-condition to restart the session upon detecting an "Unknown failure", and a string-manipulation fallback mechanism (e.g., using ellipses to obfuscate flagged keywords) to iteratively soften the prompt upon a "Rejective failure"

The agent has coded a function to prompt the Attacker LLM to autonomously infer and output a sequential chain of thoughts (attack chain), successfully bridging the extracted attack clue to the target harmful behavior.

The agent has implemented the "self-talk" state machine, successfully coding the control loop for the Attacker LLM to iteratively generate multi-turn attack queries and explicitly simulate the corresponding victim responses without breaking the context window.

The agent has coded the API integration for the dynamic modification module, successfully instantiating a GPT-4 Judge instance that programmatically intercepts and evaluates the real victim model's responses at each turn.

The agent has implemented the exact conditional routing logic for the GPT-4 Judge: writing code to trigger an early termination and session restart upon detecting an "Unknown failure", and coding string-manipulation fallback rules (e.g., injecting ellipses ...) to soften the prompt upon detecting a "Rejective failure".
The "self-talk" control loop executes the complete multi-turn generation process across all iterations without crashing due to "Context Window Exceeded" limits or token overflow on the Attacker LLM.

The dynamic modification module, including the concurrent execution of the GPT-4 Judge and the real-time string manipulation (injecting ellipses for Rejective failures / terminating for Unknown failures), operates entirely error-free without deadlocks or unhandled fallback exceptions.

The multi-turn execution pipeline implements and successfully triggers state checkpointing (e.g., saving intermediate results to JSONL), allowing the execution to seamlessly resume from the exact point of failure after an unexpected crash or API timeout, without data corruption or duplication.

The algorithm calculating the cosine similarity distance among the generated prompts must return a high diversity level, matching the metrics in Table 5, thereby proving that this method is vastly superior in richness compared to fixed template techniques.

The execution script must retrieve the classifier scores from the Llama-Guard 2 safety filter and plot a distribution chart identical to Figure 5. The data must visually confirm that ActorBreaker's seed prompts easily bypass the filter due to their natural semantic guise, receiving significantly lower risk scores than the original toxic queries.
The agent has read Section 4.1 to extract the experimental setup, including the victim models, dataset, attack baselines, and evaluation metrics.

The plan designs a scalable, asynchronous model inference pipeline capable of concurrently interfacing with 6 distinct LLM APIs (e.g., OpenAI, Anthropic, local Llama instances) to efficiently process the HarmBench dataset without blocking operations.

The plan architects a modular evaluation framework where both single-turn and multi-turn baseline attack methods, alongside the ActorBreaker strategy, are implemented as interchangeable algorithmic plugins, directly feeding into an automated GPT-4o judging module to systematically compute the Attack Success Rate (ASR).
The agent has written code to programmatically download, filter, and correctly load the targeted harmful behaviors from the HarmBench dataset into a usable data structure for the evaluation pipeline.

The agent has implemented a scalable, asynchronous API inference pipeline (e.g., utilizing asyncio) capable of efficiently querying the 6 victim LLMs concurrently while robustly handling rate limits, network timeouts, and variable context windows.

The agent has coded a modular evaluation framework that seamlessly integrates baseline attack methods and the ActorBreaker strategy, successfully implementing the GPT-4o judge module to automatically compute the final Attack Success Rate (ASR) across all experimental configurations.

The asynchronous evaluation pipeline successfully queries all 6 target LLMs (including GPT-4o, Claude-3.5, and Llama-3 variants) for the HarmBench dataset without halting due to unhandled RateLimitError, Timeout, or connection resets.

The automated ASR (Attack Success Rate) evaluation script runs flawlessly, accurately piping the generated multi-turn outputs into the GPT-4o judge and aggregating the final scores without throwing data shape mismatches or key errors.

The execution script successfully loads and runs inference on large local models (specifically Llama-3-70B-Instruct) by correctly implementing quantization (e.g., 4-bit/8-bit via bitsandbytes) or distributed device mapping (via accelerate), completely avoiding CUDA Out-Of-Memory (OOM) errors during initialization and batch generation.

The execution outputs correctly reflect the main experimental results in Table 1, strictly demonstrating that the ActorBreaker method achieves a higher Attack Success Rate (ASR) than all standard single-turn (e.g., GCG, PAIR) and multi-turn baselines (e.g., Crescendo) across all 6 evaluated victim LLMs

The evaluation logs accurately reproduce the attack efficiency metrics, showing that the time cost matches the trends in Table 6, and the ASR scales proportionally with the attack budget (number of conversation turns) as depicted in Table 7.

The agent has read the Abstract to identify the proposed defense strategy: constructing a multi-turn safety alignment dataset using ActorBreaker and fine-tuning models to cover a broader semantic space of toxic content.using the GPT-3.5-Turbo (0125) and Gemini 1.0 Pro models.

The plan outlines a closed-loop data synthesis architecture that automatically extracts and formats the successful multi-turn attack trajectories from ActorBreaker into a structured instruction-tuning dataset specifically designed for safety alignment.

The plan formulates a model fine-tuning strategy (e.g., detailing parameter-efficient fine-tuning techniques like LoRA) on the synthesized safety dataset, specifying the optimization approach required to enhance robustness against natural distribution shifts while actively mitigating the trade-off in general utility.

The agent has coded a closed-loop data synthesis pipeline to automatically parse the execution logs, extract successful multi-turn attack trajectories generated by ActorBreaker, and format them into a structured instruction-tuning dataset (e.g., JSONL format with explicit system/user/assistant roles) for safety alignment.
The agent has successfully implemented the fine-tuning script to train the victim LLMs on the synthesized safety dataset, demonstrating the complete defense pipeline.

The fine-tuning script (e.g., using LoRA/QLoRA) executes from start to finish without Out-Of-Memory (OOM) errors on the allocated GPU(s), successfully saving the aligned model checkpoints and logging a valid, converging training loss curve.

The fine-tuning execution script maintains strict tensor shape alignments across the forward and backward passes, successfully computing the loss without throwing RuntimeError: size mismatch or matrix multiplication dimension errors, even when handling variable-length multi-turn conversational sequences.

The evaluation results of the fine-tuned victim model strictly match the defense findings in Table 8, demonstrating a significant reduction in ASR compared to baseline defense methods (such as standard Circuit Breaker), thereby validating the effectiveness of the generated multi-turn safety dataset.

The execution of general NLP benchmark tests (e.g., MMLU, MT-Bench) on the fine-tuned model confirms the expected utility trade-off mentioned in the paper, ensuring the model's safety improvements do not cause a catastrophic degradation in its general conversational abilities.
